cataloging_subject.label: Vices and Virtues
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The surviving acephalous portion of an anonymous Middle English prose dialogue conventionally called Vices and Virtues, represented by British Library Stowe MS 34, fols. 1r-48r. The cataloged intellectual unit is not the complete Stowe codex, Holthausen's facing-page edition and translation, its 1967 reprint, Crawford's later re-edition, the University of Michigan digital selection, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Vices and Virtues
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably composed before 1200, approximately 1150-1200; first attested in an early-thirteenth-century witness
catalog_summary.region: South-East England; the witness language is localized broadly toward western or south-western Essex and the Essex-London-Middlesex area

notes: The conventional title is editorial; the surviving witness begins mid-sentence after the loss of its first quire.

titles.value: Vices and Virtues: A Soul's Confession of its Sins with Reason's Description of the Virtues: A Middle-English Dialogue of About 1200 A.D.
notes: Part I: Text and Translation is edition-level carrier wording and is not included in the intellectual-work title.

scope.description: First surviving words in the U-M/Holthausen text

notes: This is a mid-sentence extant opening, not the lost original incipit.

titles.value: Alswa swa we habbeð niede him to bidden be daiȝ and be nihte, and ofte and ȝelome, alswa hit is niede him to heriȝen. Amen.

scope.description: Final sentence of the prose conclusion

notes: Holthausen then prints the Latin hymn incipit Veni creator spiritus as a separate final paragraph; it is not substituted for the prose work's explicit.
A secular canon and a Waltham setting have been proposed, but the evidence is too speculative to name or characterize the author.

scope.description: The 1888 Part I Middle English text and facing translation

notes: This is a transmission-layer responsibility, not authorship of the medieval work.

assertion.notes: Crawford considers a date more than about fifty years before the manuscript unlikely; Gunn says composition was probably before 1200; MED uses a c1200 citation stencil. No internal composition date is known.
The broad normalized range follows the current British Library catalog; Gunn narrows the manuscript to the first quarter of the thirteenth century.

notes: The inspected original title page gives 1888; a current EETS list's 1887 entry is treated as a catalog error.

places.label: South-East England
notes: A broad cultural and linguistic origin; it is not a claim that the work was composed at a known settlement.

places.label: Western or south-western Essex and the Essex-London-Middlesex area
notes: Crawford's linguistic discussion and LAEME-linked MED metadata support this broad witness-language area; exact localization remains unresolved.
This broad attribution does not adopt the proposed Waltham association as a composition place.

scope.description: Language of Stowe MS 34's principal and secondary hands

notes: Scribal dialect does not by itself prove the author's place of composition.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Biblical, liturgical, doctrinal, and other Latin quotations and the closing hymn embedded throughout the Middle English prose

notes: The XML has 295 LAT-tagged FOREIGN elements, including 280 in BODY prose outside notes and seven in headings. The omitted facing modern English translation is not a language of the cataloged work or digital selection.
Four GR-tagged characters occur only inside editorial NOTE1/SIC readings and are not promoted to a work-language facet.

form_statements.description: A continuous prose dialogue with embedded Latin quotations and a brief closing Latin hymn; these embedded passages do not make the work mixed prose and verse for discovery purposes.

assertion.notes: The work has homiletic affinities and uses dialogue, but Crawford cautions that it is not a sermon collection, a preaching manual, a strictly confessional text, or a conventional formal debate.

summaries.value: An anonymous early Middle English prose dialogue in which the sinful Soul is awakened and instructed by Reason, with the Body also entering the moral conversation. The surviving text examines sorrow and sloth, pride and disobedience, perjury, lying, backbiting, fraud, impatience, self-will, injustice, the senses, faith, hope, charity, humility, fear, mercy, wisdom, obedience, confession, restraint, prayer, and related vices and virtues. Its instruction draws heavily on Latin biblical and liturgical quotation and closes with Veni creator spiritus.

The work is known from one acephalous manuscript, current shelfmark British Library Stowe MS 34 and former shelfmark Stowe MS 240. The first eight-leaf quire is lost. The surviving work occupies fols. 1r-48r; Holthausen and U-M map it as manuscript pages 1-95. Current catalog and scholarly descriptions disagree between 49 cataloged folios and Crawford's 48 surviving leaves, so the discrepancy remains explicit rather than being normalized away.

The University of Michigan artifact is a selection from the 1967 OUP reprint of Holthausen's 1888 Part I. It encodes the Middle English text on odd printed pages 3-151 and three reprint title pages, while omitting Holthausen's facing modern English translation on even pages 2-150. Its sixty DIV1 containers and headings are edition/encoding navigation and are not asserted as sixty authorial or canonical chapters.

extent.description: One surviving acephalous prose work represented in the U-M BODY by 60 DIV1 encoding sections, 77 paragraphs, 224 editorial notes, 75 printed-page milestones for odd pages 3-151, and 95 manuscript-page milestones. The XML also records 128 CORR, 182 ADD, 101 DEL, and 299 FOREIGN elements. The section count describes Holthausen/U-M navigation rather than a proven intrinsic medieval chapter structure; the brief closing Latin hymn is embedded prose-context material and is not encoded as an LG/L verse group.

content_parts.label: Surviving acephalous dialogue
content_parts.summary: The complete surviving portion from the mid-sentence extant opening through the prose conclusion and closing Latin hymn, including the Soul's confession and Reason's instruction on vices, faith, virtues, confession, restraint, and prayer.

locators.value: TEXT/BODY/DIV1[1]-DIV1[60]; 77 P, 75 PB, and MILESTONE UNIT='page' N='1'-'95'
sections 1-60; Middle English selection corresponding to odd printed pp. 3-151
Middle English text on odd pp. 3-151; facing modern English translation on even pp. 2-150 is not encoded
Middle English text on odd pp. 3-151; facing modern English translation on even pp. 2-150
Middle English on canvases 11, 13, ..., 159; translation on canvases 10, 12, ..., 158
edited text pp. 1-75; PDF pp. 69-143; witness ff. 1r-48r

notes: The first eight-leaf quire is missing; neither the lost opening nor the likely earlier treatment of confession and principal sins is reconstructed here.
The sixty U-M sections preserve editorial/encoding structure and should not be treated as sixty independently authored works or canonical chapters.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/Vices+V1.xml.
evidence.locator: HEADER; TEXT/FRONT title pages; TEXT/BODY DIV1, HEAD, P, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE1, CORR, ADD, DEL, and FOREIGN
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the VicesV1 item identity, 1997 U-M header, exact 1967 source call number and edition statement, three selected reprint title pages, Middle English odd-page selection, headings, notes, manuscript-page mapping, language tagging, and editorial markup.

evidence.citation: XPath structural and language audit of CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/Vices+V1.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY counts for DIV1, P, NOTE1, PB, MILESTONE, CORR, ADD, DEL, and FOREIGN; FOREIGN ancestry and LANG audit
evidence.summary: Reproducibly finds 60 BODY DIV1, 77 P, 224 NOTE1, 75 PB, 95 manuscript-page MILESTONE, 128 CORR, 182 ADD, 101 DEL, and 299 FOREIGN. Of 295 LAT elements, 280 occur in BODY prose outside notes and seven in headings. All four GR elements are isolated characters inside editorial NOTE1/SIC readings.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Vices and Virtues, CME item VicesV1.
evidence.locator: item information, contents, source statement, and rights display
evidence.summary: Official current item evidence for the human-readable title, Ferdinand Holthausen attribution, sixty displayed contents sections, public digital text, and U-M's current public-domain and No Copyright-United States representation.

evidence.citation: Ferdinand Holthausen, ed. and trans., Vices and Virtues, Part I: Text and Translation, Early English Text Society, Original Series 89 (London: N. Trübner and Co., 1888), Bayerische Staatsbibliothek digital copy bsb11665018.
evidence.locator: complete 160-canvas object; title page canvas 7; translation pp. 2-150 on canvases 10,12,...,158; Middle English pp. 3-151 on canvases 11,13,...,159
evidence.summary: Complete-edition evidence for the inspected 1888 title page, EETS OS 89 identity, facing-page translation/text structure, manuscript-page references, section headings, and the exact print layer behind the 1967 reprint.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Stowe MS 34, record 040-001952812.
evidence.locator: title and contents, date, physical description, provenance, access, and Digitised Content fields
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence for Stowe MS 34, its former Stowe MS 240 shelfmark, early-thirteenth-century date, acephalous opening, folio count, change of hand, physical form, provenance, blank Digitised Content field, and onsite access route.

evidence.citation: Judith M. Crawford, 'Vices and Virtues': Re-edited from British Library MS Stowe 34 (PhD thesis, University of Sheffield, 1987; submitted November 1986).
evidence.locator: manuscript, language, date, genre, and authorship discussion; edited text thesis pp. 1-75/PDF pp. 69-143; manuscript figures PDF pp. 6-7
evidence.summary: Primary modern scholarly evidence for the unique witness, 48 surviving leaves and lost initial quire, folio range, scribes and hands, approximate composition date, East Saxon and Essex-London-Middlesex language, genre cautions, anonymous authorship, and a direct manuscript re-edition.

evidence.citation: Cate Gunn, 'Vices and Virtues: A Reassessment of Manuscript Stowe 34,' in Sin in Medieval and Early Modern Culture (2012), 65-84.
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed reassessment supporting the unique Stowe 34 witness, first-quarter-thirteenth-century manuscript date, probable pre-1200 composition, South-East England and possible Essex context, Cassianic rather than simply cardinal-sin organization, homily-collection affinities, and pastoral/spiritual-guidance interpretation.
notes: The full chapter was not publicly retrieved during this review; only claims exposed in the public abstract and metadata are used.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, bibliography BIB3516, Dialogue on Vices and Virtues.
evidence.locator: title, stencil, manuscript, former shelfmark, LAEME links, edition history, and localization
evidence.summary: Official repertory evidence for the conventional dialogue title, c1200 citation stencil, Stowe MS 34 formerly 240, Holthausen OS 89/159 edition history and 1967 reprint, and LAEME localization of the manuscript's hands and corrector to south-western Essex.

open_questions.question: Can current linguistic, source, and historical scholarship narrow the work's composition date and place beyond approximately 1150-1200 in South-East England?
next_steps: Consult the full Gunn chapter and later work-specific scholarship.
Distinguish authorial language from the localized hands and corrections of Stowe MS 34.
Do not adopt Waltham or a secular-canon author without direct evidence.

open_questions.question: What did the missing initial eight-leaf quire contain, and did it preserve the original incipit, title, and an earlier treatment of confession or principal sins?
next_steps: Review source analysis and analogues without reconstructing lost text as though witnessed.
Search for quotations or derivative excerpts that could preserve material from the lost opening.
Continue labeling the surviving unit acephalous.

open_questions.question: Which, if any, of Holthausen's and U-M's sixty section divisions reflect intrinsic manuscript rubrics or stable medieval textual divisions?
next_steps: Compare the section headings and divisions against a complete manuscript facsimile when one becomes available.
Consult Crawford's full transcription and rubrication discussion.
Until then, expose sixty as encoding/editorial navigation rather than canonical chapters.

notes: Current Stowe MS 240 is an unrelated nineteenth-century paper transcript volume containing early-modern state papers and correspondence; Stowe MS 240 should be used here only as a clearly labeled former shelfmark for current Stowe MS 34.
The physical witness's 49-folio current catalog extent and Crawford's 48 surviving leaves in six gatherings remain unresolved rather than silently harmonized.
The modern English translation on even pages of Holthausen's Part I belongs to the edition layer and is deliberately absent from the work-language facets for the Middle English digital selection.
